{{Short description="Theorem connecting symmetries and conservation laws in physics"}}
{{Infobox physical law
| name = Noether's theorem
| image = Emmy Noether.jpg
| caption = [[Emmy Noether]] (1882–1935), who proved the theorem in 1915 and published it in 1918
| formula = Every differentiable symmetry of the action has a corresponding conservation law.
| discovered_by = [[Emmy Noether]] (1918)
}}

'''Noether's theorem''' (or '''Noether's first theorem''') states that every [[differentiable function|differentiable]] symmetry of the [[action (physics)|action]] of a [[physical system]] with [[conservative force]]s has a corresponding [[conservation law]]. The theorem was proven by mathematician [[Emmy Noether]] in 1915 and published in 1918.

The action of a physical system is the [[time integral|integral over time]] of a [[Lagrangian mechanics|Lagrangian]] function, from which the system's behavior can be determined by the [[principle of least action]]. Noether's theorem is used in [[theoretical physics]] and the [[calculus of variations]].

== Statement ==
For every continuous [[symmetry (physics)|symmetry]] of the [[action (physics)|action]], there exists a conserved quantity (a constant of the motion).

Mathematically: If the Lagrangian <math>L(q, \dot{q}, t)</math> is invariant under an infinitesimal transformation:
:<math>q_i \to q_i + \varepsilon f_i(q, \dot{q}, t)</math>
then the quantity
:<math>J = \sum_i \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} f_i</math>
is conserved: <math>\frac{dJ}{dt} = 0</math>.

== Fundamental correspondences ==

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Symmetry !! Conserved quantity
|-
| [[Time translation symmetry|Time translation invariance]] || [[Conservation of energy|Energy]]
|-
| [[Translational symmetry|Spatial translation invariance]] || [[Conservation of momentum|Linear momentum]]
|-
| [[Rotational symmetry|Rotational invariance]] || [[Conservation of angular momentum|Angular momentum]]
|-
| [[Gauge invariance]] || [[Charge conservation|Electric charge]]
|-
| Spatial reflection (parity) || [[Parity (physics)]]
|}

== Physical examples ==

=== Energy conservation ===
If the Lagrangian has no explicit time dependence (<math>\partial L/\partial t = 0</math>), the Hamiltonian (total energy) is conserved:
:<math>H = \sum_i p_i \dot{q}_i - L = E = \text{constant}</math>

=== Momentum conservation ===
If the Lagrangian is invariant under spatial translations (<math>q_i \to q_i + \varepsilon</math>), the conjugate momentum is conserved:
:<math>p = \frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}} = \text{constant}</math>

=== Angular momentum conservation ===
If the system is rotationally invariant, angular momentum is conserved. In a central potential <math>V(r)</math>, <math>\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{p} = \text{constant}</math>.

== Historical significance ==
Noether's theorem elevated conservation laws from empirical observations to consequences of fundamental symmetries of nature. [[Albert Einstein]] described Noether as "the most important woman in the history of mathematics."

The theorem is a cornerstone of modern theoretical physics, underpinning:
* [[Classical mechanics]]
* [[Quantum field theory]]
* [[General relativity]]
* [[Gauge theory]]

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Conservation law]]
* [[Symmetry (physics)]]
* [[Lagrangian mechanics]]
* [[Conservation of energy]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Noether's theorem| ]]
[[Category:Conservation laws]]
[[Category:Symmetry]]
[[Category:Theoretical physics]]
